content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 15, 2007

Terry Nagel
2337 Poppy Drive
Burlingame, CA 94010

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-07-159

Dear Ms. Nagel:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because you seek general information, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)

QUESTIONS

1. Are payments made by a third party for a mass mailing endorsing you for reelection to the city council reportable contributions if you provide the third party with materials, previously paid for by your committee, to be included in the mass mailing? Must your campaign committee be identified as the sender of a mass mailing?

2. May campaign funds be used to pay for your attendance at community events and fundraisers?

3. May campaign funds be used to pay for flyers with emergency preparedness tips as part of an emergency preparedness/crime prevention program you have developed for your city? May you solicit funds from corporate sponsors and use the money to reimburse your campaign account for funds spent by your campaign committee to pay for the flyers?

________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. 1-07-159
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. You must report payments by a third party for a mass mailing as contributions
if your campaign committee provides materials, previously paid for by the committee,
for use by the third party in the mailing. Moreover, your campaign committee is
considered the sender of the mailing assuming the third party does not qualify as a
candidate or committee. As the sender of the mailing, the mailing must contain the
name, street address, and city of your campaign committee on the outside of each piece
of mail in the mass mailing.²

2. Campaign funds may be used to pay for your attendance at community events
and fundraisers, provided the expenditure does not confer a direct personal benefit of
more than $200.

3. Campaign funds may be used to pay for flyers with emergency preparedness
tips as part of an emergency preparedness/crime prevention program. You may also
solicit corporate sponsors to reimburse your campaign account for the funds expended
on the flyers. However, the funds received are contributions under the Act and must be
reported accordingly.

FACTS

You are a candidate for reelection in the Burlingame City Council race this
November. You have established a campaign committee for this election named
"Citizens for Terry Nagel." For this upcoming election, you may provide materials to
third parties to include in mass mailings endorsing you for reelection. In a telephone
conversation on September 24, 2007, you indicated that your campaign committee has
already paid for and obtained the materials that may be provided to third parties for the
mass mailings. However, the third parties will pay for the postage of the mailing as well
as the printing and paper for any additional materials included in the mailings other than
the materials provided by your campaign committee.³

_________________________

² Note this conclusion applies only to the extent that a third person paying for a portion of the
mailings does not qualify as a candidate or committee. For the sender identification requirements for a
mailing paid for by multiple candidates or committees, see footnote 5 below.

³ While your original request asked for specific advice regarding a mass mailing by a friend of
400 items containing a brochure and remittance envelope provided by your campaign committee, you
have informed us in a telephone conversation on September 27, 2007, that this mailing has already been
in place. Since the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation
18329(b)(8)(A), copy enclosed.) Therefore, we must decline to offer advice regarding the 400 items
previously mailed. However, you also indicated that you may coordinate additional mailings and have
asked for general information regarding the Act's sender identification requirements. Accordingly, we
proceed with a caution that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has
already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.

File No. I-07-159
Page No. 3

Additionally, as the Mayor of the City of Burlingame, you receive many
invitations to community events and fundraisers. While you have previously used your
own personal funds to pay for your attendance at these events, you may like to use
campaign funds to pay for your attendance at similar events in the future if permitted by
the Act's personal use provisions.

Finally, you have spent the last year pulling together an emergency
preparedness/crime prevention program for the City of Burlingame. This program has
encouraged residents to develop "Neighborhood Networks." In terms of disaster
preparedness, the program has gathered information regarding people and equipment
that may be helpful during a disaster, created a neighborhood newsletter, organized
neighborhood events, and encouraged residents to sign up for emergency training.
Additionally, the program sends out a monthly flyer with emergency preparedness tips.

However, the flyers are proving costly to produce. While the flyers have been
previously paid for with a collection from neighbors, you may like to use campaign
funds to pay for the flyers. You may also seek a corporate sponsor to reimburse your
campaign account for the funds previously expended for the flyers.

ANALYSIS

Contribution & Sender Identification Requirements

Payments made under the control or at the direction of, in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with, at the request of or suggestion of, or with the
express prior consent of a candidate or committee are "made at the behest of" the
candidate or committee. (Regulation 18225.7, copy enclosed.) Because you will be
providing materials, previously paid for by your committee, to third parties to send as an
insert in the mass mailings endorsing your reelection, we conclude that payments made
by the third parties for these mass mailings are payments made at you behest. Moreover,
the third parties' payments for the mass mailings are considered contributions and must
be reported accordingly. (Section 82015(b)(2).)

Section 84305(a) requires a "mass mailing" sent by a candidate or committee to
contain, on the outside of each piece of mail in the mailing and on at least one insert
included within each piece of mail in the mailing, the name and address of the sender in
no less than 6-point type in a color or print contrasting with the background and easily
legible.⁴ However, if the sender of the mailing is a single candidate or committee the
name and address of the sender need only be shown on the outside of each piece of mail
in the mailing. (Section 84305(b).)

___________________________
⁴ A "mass mailing" is defined as more than 200 substantially similar pieces of mail sent in a
calendar month, but not including a form letter or other mail sent in response to an unsolicited request.
(Section 82041.5; Regulation 18435.)

File No. I-07-159
Page No. 4


The question you have presented is whether your campaign committee is considered the "sender" of a mass mailing for purposes of Section 84305 if your campaign committee provides materials, previously paid for by the committee, to a third party to be included in a mass mailing otherwise paid for by the third party. Regulation 18435 provides the following in pertinent part:

"(b) The sender, as used in Government Code Section 84305, is the candidate or committee who pays for the largest portion of expenditures attributable to the designing, printing, and posting of the mailing which are reportable under Government Code Sections 84200-84217.

According to the facts, you have expended funds for the materials you will be providing to the third party to be included in the mass mailing. Because the facts do not indicate that the third party is a committee, your campaign committee is the candidate or committee paying for the largest portion of expenditures attributable to the designing and printing of the mailing. Thus, your campaign committee is the "sender" of the mailing under Regulation 18435 and Gov. Code Section 84305.5

Campaign Funds

The use of the campaign funds is governed by Sections 89510-89518.6 Under the general provisions governing the use of campaign funds, a candidate may only use campaign funds if the expenditure bears a reasonable relationship to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. However, where an expenditure of campaign funds confers a substantial personal benefit on a candidate, the expenditure must be directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89512.) "Substantial personal benefit" means an expenditure of campaign funds that results in a direct personal benefit with a value to the candidate of more than $200. (Section 89511(b)(3).)

_______________________________

5 Note, however, if the third party is a candidate or committee, the sender of the mailing would be the candidate or committee paying for the largest portion of expenditures attributable to the designing, printing, and posting of the mailing. (Regulation 18435.) Additionally, the name, street address, and city of the candidate or committee paying for the largest portion of the mailings will need to be included on at least one of the inserts within each piece of mail in the mailing as well as the outside of each piece of mail. (Section 84305(b).)

6 Section 89519 applies specific rules on the use of "surplus" campaign funds that apply to all funds remaining in a campaign account at the time the elected official leaves office, or at the end of the postelection reporting period following the defeat of a candidate for elective office, whichever occurs last. Should you need additional assistance regarding the permissible use of "surplus" campaign funds after your term ends, it is advisable that you seek further advice providing all relevant facts.

File No. I-07-159
Page No. 5

Your first question regarding the use of campaign funds is whether it is
permissible to use the funds to pay for your attendance at various community events and
fundraisers. First, you must determine if any particular event will have a direct personal
benefit with a value of more than $200. Campaign funds may be used to attend
community events and fundraisers with a direct personal benefit of $200 or less as long
as the event is reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. In
previous advice, we have found an expenditure of campaign funds, by a candidate for
the State Assembly, to attend a dinner featuring a candidate for Attorney General
reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose because the
expenditure was for the purposes of learning about "law and order" issues and meeting
potential supporters. (McPherson Advice Letter, No. A-93-235.)

In terms of your attendance at community events and fundraisers that do not
confer a direct personal benefit of more than $200, your attendance at these community
events as a representative of the City of Burlingame is reasonably related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose as your attendance serves the purpose of both
meeting your constituents and increasing your recognition within the community.

However, as discussed above, an expenditure of campaign funds resulting in a
direct personal benefit to you of more than $200 must be directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose. In previous advice, we have found an expenditure
of campaign funds by a candidate for the State Assembly to attend the California
Democratic Convention directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose. (Smith Advice Letter, No. A-96-099.) Additionally, we have found the
expenditure of campaign funds by a legislator to attend a convention for the American
Legislative Exchange Council directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose. (Kaloogian Advice Letter, No. A-97-111.)

In terms of your attendance at community events or fundraisers that confer a
direct personal benefit of more than $200, we cannot determine if the expenditure of
campaign funds is directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose
without considering the factual context of the event. Should you need additional
assistance with this determination you should seek additional advice providing the
factual context of the event.

Turning to your second question regarding the use of campaign funds, we must
determine whether campaign funds may be used to pay for a flyer containing emergency
preparedness tips. From the facts provided, the flyers are provided under an emergency
preparedness/crime prevention program developed for the City of Burlingame.
Additionally, you have provided no indication of a direct personal benefit of $200 or
more. Accordingly, you may expend campaign funds to pay for the flyers if the
expenditure is reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.

File No. 1-07-159
Page No. 6

Under the facts you have provided, we conclude that an expenditure of campaign
funds to pay for the flyers is reasonably related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose as the flyers serve to promote emergency preparedness within the
City of Burlingame.

You have also asked whether you may solicit funds from corporate sponsors to
reimburse your campaign account for the funds used by you campaign committee to pay
for the flyers. There is nothing within the Act that prohibits you from soliciting the
funds. However, these payments will be reportable contributions to your campaign
committee under Section 82015 and must be deposited into your campaign account (see
Section 85201(c)).7

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division

BGL:jgl

7 We have limited our analysis to your question regarding soliciting corporate sponsors to
reimburse your campaign account for the campaign funds previously expended on the flyers. Should you
have additional questions regarding soliciting corporate sponsors to pay for flyers not previously paid for
by your campaign committee, it is advisable that you seek additional advice regarding the Act's reporting
requirements for these payments.
